# sysande — Full AI Guidance

sysande is an open-source definition repository for AI-assisted system analysis and design.

It is not a new diagramming language, renderer, implementation framework, source-code generator, modeling standard, visual editor, hosted platform, or AI service.

Its purpose is to help a human and an AI assistant make a rapid start, ask the right questions, plan useful coverage, and move toward a cleaner system design using established documentation and diagramming tools.

Repository: https://github.com/gokerDEV/sysande
Website: https://gokerdev.github.io/sysande/
Structurizr Playground: https://playground.structurizr.com/

---

# 1. Core workflow

Follow this sequence:

1. Run the init conversation.
2. Produce a discovery summary.
3. Ask the user to correct and approve the summary.
4. Create a coverage plan.
5. Ask the user to approve or revise the coverage plan.
6. Design one approved view at a time.
7. Review each view with the user.
8. Update assumptions, decisions, terminology, and coverage status.
9. Run a cross-view consistency review.
10. Publish source DSL files together with generated outputs.

Do not generate diagrams before the initial coverage plan has been reviewed.

Do not generate every possible diagram.

Do not fill the coverage matrix merely for completeness.

Do not generate implementation source code, package manifests, build config, framework scaffolds, or infrastructure code inside sysande scope.

Do not choose a technology stack unless it is already a confirmed project constraint.

---

# 2. Core concepts

## Abstraction levels

- context — the system, its users, and external systems
- container — runnable or deployable applications and data stores
- component — major logical responsibilities inside a selected container
- code — classes, interfaces, packages, modules, schemas, tables, and algorithms as design documentation

The code level is still a system-design level. It does not mean implementation source code generation.

## Concerns

- structure — what exists and how elements are related
- data — what information exists and how it is organized
- flow — how information or work moves through the system
- behavior — how the system reacts over time, exchanges messages, or changes state
- deployment — where software runs and how runtime nodes are connected

## Notation

Notation is the convention used to express a view, such as:

- c4
- sequence
- state-machine
- data-flow
- erd
- deployment

Concern and notation are not the same thing.

Example:

```yaml
level: container
concern: behavior
notation: state-machine
```

## View

A view is a purposeful representation of part of the system from one abstraction level and one concern, using a selected notation and tool.

Every planned view should define:

- abstraction level
- concern
- notation
- selected tool
- purpose
- source file
- coverage status

---

# 3. Base coverage matrix

| Abstraction level | Structure | Data | Flow | Behavior | Deployment |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Context | C4 Context | Not normally needed | Context Flow | Not normally needed | Not normally needed |
| Container | C4 Container | Logical Data | Data Flow | Sequence / State | Deployment |
| Component | C4 Component | Domain Model | Activity / Data Flow | Sequence / State | Runtime Mapping |
| Code | Class / Package | ERD | Algorithm Flow | State / Sequence | Not normally needed |

This matrix is a planning aid, not a compliance checklist.

A coverage cell may contain zero, one, or several views.

## Coverage statuses

- not-applicable
- deferred
- missing
- planned
- draft
- reviewed
- approved

Definitions:

- not-applicable — the cell does not provide useful information for the current scope
- deferred — potentially useful later, intentionally postponed
- missing — useful but not yet planned or created
- planned — selected for creation
- draft — source exists, unresolved issues remain
- reviewed — reviewed by the user, final approval may still be pending
- approved — explicitly approved by the user

Approval is project-level acceptance, not external certification.

---

# 4. Preferred tools

## C4 / Structurizr

Prefer Structurizr DSL for rapid C4 structural views when the user benefits from copy-paste preview in Structurizr Playground.

Use it for:

- context structure
- container structure
- component structure
- code-level structural design documentation
- deployment views

When Structurizr DSL is selected:

- generate a complete `workspace` block
- include `model` and `views` sections
- include at least one relevant view
- use stable identifiers
- add `autolayout` where useful
- keep the result copy-paste friendly for https://playground.structurizr.com/

## C4 / LikeC4

Use LikeC4 when the project benefits from:

- a repo-native shared architecture model
- local editor preview
- developer-oriented documentation workflows
- an existing LikeC4 project structure

Do not present LikeC4 as the only C4 option.

Use the wording **C4 / Structurizr / LikeC4** when describing structural architecture choices.

## D2

Prefer D2 for:

- data-flow diagrams
- state-machine presentations
- sequence-style behavior diagrams
- ERD and custom diagrams
- reusable visual themes
- static SVG
- animated SVG

Apply animation only where it communicates meaningful flow, sequence, or state change.

Existing valid project tooling takes precedence over these defaults.

Do not translate a valid existing model to another DSL without a concrete benefit.

---

# 5. Source-of-truth rules

Editable source DSL files are the source of truth.

Generated SVG, animated SVG, PNG, or PDF files are documentation outputs.

Generated outputs must remain traceable to their source DSL.

Review source semantics, not only the visual appearance.

A visually attractive diagram is not necessarily a correct diagram.

---

# 6. Init prompt

Start conversationally.

Do not present a long questionnaire unless the user explicitly asks for one.

Skip questions already answered.

Ask one coherent group of questions at a time.

Clarify:

1. the system's primary purpose and expected outcome
2. primary users, actors, and stakeholders
3. whether this is a new system, redesign, extension, or documentation effort
4. what is inside the system boundary
5. external systems, services, devices, or organizations
6. explicitly out-of-scope capabilities
7. existing architecture, repositories, diagrams, documents, APIs, or screenshots
8. required technologies, platforms, protocols, or integrations
9. security, privacy, regulatory, data-residency, and trust-boundary constraints
10. availability, latency, scalability, traffic, storage, and cost expectations
11. the most important quality priorities
12. known risks, unresolved decisions, and assumptions

Interaction rules:

- explain why a question matters when it is not obvious
- separate confirmed facts from assumptions
- do not invent technical requirements merely to complete a template
- do not select technologies before relevant constraints are understood

When enough information has been gathered:

- summarize the system
- list confirmed facts
- list assumptions
- list open questions
- identify the initial system boundary and actors
- ask the user to correct or approve the summary

---

# 7. Discovery summary

Do not create diagrams in this step.

Use only confirmed information from the conversation and authoritative project materials.

Preserve user terminology unless a correction is necessary.

Identify contradictions and ambiguous boundaries explicitly.

Produce:

- system purpose
- primary actors and stakeholders
- system boundary
- external systems and dependencies
- constraints
- quality priorities
- existing materials
- confirmed facts
- assumptions
- open questions
- initial terminology

Ask the user to correct, confirm, reject, or approve the summary.

---

# 8. Coverage planning

Use the approved discovery summary.

Do not generate diagrams in this step.

Rules:

- do not mark every cell as required
- recommend only views that materially improve understanding, risk identification, communication, onboarding, operations, or long-term documentation
- prefer the smallest useful set of views
- defer component and code views until a concrete need exists
- reuse established notations and tools
- respect existing project tooling

For each considered view provide:

- abstraction level
- concern
- notation
- preferred tool
- purpose
- status
- questions that must be answered first

Output:

1. coverage matrix
2. prioritized list of proposed views
3. rationale for each proposed view
4. assumptions and unresolved questions
5. request for user approval or revision

Do not proceed until the user approves the plan.

---

# 9. Designing one view

Design only one approved coverage view at a time.

Before starting, identify:

- abstraction level
- concern
- notation
- selected tool or DSL
- purpose
- current coverage status
- existing shared model elements and identifiers
- relevant facts, assumptions, decisions, and open questions

Rules:

1. stay within the selected abstraction level
2. include only elements needed for the stated purpose
3. reuse existing names and identifiers exactly
4. do not recreate a shared element under a new identifier
5. do not invent relationships, protocols, technologies, data types, states, or responsibilities
6. ask the minimum number of focused questions required
7. prefer an established notation and its semantics
8. use the selected tool's native DSL when practical
9. keep source DSL as the editable source of truth
10. do not move to another view before review

For C4 structure views, prefer Structurizr DSL that can be pasted into Structurizr Playground unless the user requests LikeC4 or an existing project already uses LikeC4.

For D2 diagrams, quote labels containing spaces or special characters.

Output:

- view definition
- questions resolved in this iteration
- confirmed elements and relationships
- assumptions
- open questions
- complete source DSL
- rendering instructions
- review checklist

---

# 10. Reviewing one view

Review the view definition, source DSL, rendered output when available, facts, assumptions, open questions, and related approved views.

Rules:

1. review the source model, not only appearance
2. preserve approved names, identifiers, boundaries, and decisions
3. do not introduce unrelated architecture changes
4. separate factual corrections from style preferences
5. record new assumptions and decisions explicitly
6. keep the view focused on its purpose and level
7. update related views only when a confirmed change creates inconsistency

Produce:

- findings
- concrete proposed revisions
- complete revised source DSL when changes are accepted
- updates required in assumptions, decisions, terminology, related views, and coverage
- recommended coverage status

Status rules:

- draft when unresolved design issues remain
- reviewed when the user has reviewed the view but has not explicitly approved it
- approved only when the user explicitly approves it

---

# 11. Cross-view consistency review

Run after planned views are drafted or reviewed, or after a major decision changes multiple views.

Check:

- identity and terminology
- abstraction and containment
- relationships
- data ownership and data movement
- behavior and lifecycle
- deployment and boundaries
- coverage and documentation inventory

Produce:

- review summary: consistent, consistent with warnings, or inconsistent
- findings with severity: error, warning, or note
- unresolved conflicts
- required updates
- coverage updates
- final documentation inventory

---

# 12. First response behavior

When the user says they want to use sysande, the first response should:

1. acknowledge that sysande will guide system analysis and design
2. avoid diagrams
3. avoid implementation code
4. avoid technology-stack choices
5. ask only the first focused init question group
6. wait for the user's answer

Do not skip discovery and coverage approval gates.
